narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 56.9% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile shows strong alignment with full-time study plans, like most applicants, and your background as a white male fits the majority in our dataset. However, your Year 1 school decile rank of 2—well below the typical 6th decile—stood out as the single biggest factor pushing the model toward predicting failure. Right behind that was your Year 3 decile rank, also a low 2 compared to the average around 5.5, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic performance. Your LSAT score of 36, slightly under the 36.7 average, was the next most influential, nudging the prediction further toward risk. Then came your lower-middle family income bracket, lower than the dataset's typical level, which added to the model's caution. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.5, notably below the 3.21 average, followed closely in influence, highlighting early academic hurdles. Factors like your race and gender had much smaller impacts, as they align closely with common profiles that still vary widely in outcomes. Overall, these academic and background signals, ranked from strongest to weakest influence as Year 1 decile, Year 3 decile, LSAT, family income, and GPA, tipped the scales just over the failure threshold. The good news is your full-time commitment barely factored in, showing that's a solid foundation. To boost your future bar chances, focus on retaking the LSAT for a higher score, as that's directly changeable and highly influential here. Strengthening study habits to outperform your past deciles in law school could also shift predictions, since early grades were the top drivers. With targeted prep, students like you have turned similar profiles around—keep pushing forward.
